Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the development of sustainable heating solutions, which are essential for achieving affordable and clean energy in the built environment.
Keywords: Sustainable heating, solar-thermal technologies, heat networks, CO2 savings, Energy transition
Specific Methods: Comparative case study, calculations, analysis of heating systems

Title: The Role of Identity in Coal Phase-out Transitions For the German cases of Lusatia and North Rhine-Westphalia
Abstract/Summary: This research aims to address the role of identity in the coal phase-out in the German regions North Rhine-Westphalia and Lusatia. It does so by using the framework as provided by Sanz-Hernández (2020) as a guide. Interviews were conducted with eleven regional experts, among which scientists, a mining employee, and a mayor. To complement the data gathered through these interviews, news articles were consulted in a structured way. This data was analyzed with NVIVO. As a result, it was found that especially economic; territorial; social, emotional and cultural dependence; and injustice played a significant role. Additionally, the attributes of identity do not function independently, and influence each other in different ways.

Title: REGREENING VERSUS LOCAL CLIMATE CHANGE HOW VEGETATION ACTIVITY ALTERATIONS RELATE TO LOCAL HYDRO-METEOROLOGY IN THE MEDITERRANEAN BASIN.
Abstract/Summary: In the Mediterranean, heatwaves and drought intensity and occurrence are projected to increase. As some studies and theories suggest a cooling and wetting effect of vegetation activity, this study aimed to gain a better understanding of how vegetation relates to the local climate of the Mediterranean Basin. The research was conducted by means of a remote-sensing data analysis. The change from 1982-2020 of the NOAA ANVHRR NDVI was correlated with changes of hydro-meteorological variables of the ERA5-Land dataset or the same period. Results shows positive relationships between both precipitation and temperature with vegetation activity, and hint at a potential mitigating effect of droughts by regreening. However, the complexity of the interplay between the variables impedes drawing hard conclusions, and further research is suggested.
Keywords: Mediterranean, drought, heatwave, vegetation, regreening

Title: Ecologically Sound Urban Water Governance
Abstract/Summary: Nowadays urban water governance is becoming more and more important. However, it is unknown how to effectively include ecology in urban water governance because ecology is a relatively new subject. There are however cities across the world that seem to be doing relatively good on the subject of ecological water governance. These cities can teach us how to effectively implement ecology in urban water governance. This comparison has shown that there are a couple points that seem to be very important when it comes to ecologically sound urban water governance. Collaboration, production and sharing of cohesive knowledge, authority, and the presence of agents of change are important indicators of ecologically sound urban water governance.
Keywords: Urban Water Governance, Ecologically Sound, Urban Ecology, Urbanization, Systems Thinking

Title: A Comparative Life Cycle Analysis of The Modern Milkman’s Reusable Glass Milk Bottle
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the life cycle impacts of The Modern Milkman’s reusable glass milk bottle compared to single-use alternatives. The aim is to assess the environmental benefits of reusable packaging in the dairy sector. A life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology is employed to analyze various impact categories, including production, distribution, and end-of-life scenarios. The findings indicate that reusable glass bottles significantly reduce environmental impacts when return rates are optimized and electric vehicles are utilized for distribution.
Main Research Question: How does the life cycle impact of The Modern Milkman’s reusable glass milk bottle compare to that of single-use alternatives?
Justification for SDG: This research supports SDG 12 by promoting sustainable consumption patterns through the analysis of reusable packaging solutions.

Title: Equitable transformation, transforming equality: A grounded theory study on the role of equality and equity in research and policies for transformative change
Abstract/Summary: This study aimed to explore the role that equality and equity plays in transformations research, and how it can be integrated further by asking the research question How are the issues of equality and equity incorporated into the Transformative Innovation Policy conceptual framework and practice, and what lessons can be derived from it? Through working in collaboration with the Transformative Innovation Policy Consortium, data was collected on how researchers perceived and implemented equality and equity in their policy work. It was found that equality and equity was integral to TIP, although in an implicit manner through procedural mechanisms such as co-creation and mutual learning.
Main Research Question: How are the issues of equality and equity incorporated into the Transformative Innovation Policy conceptual framework and practice, and what lessons can be derived from it?
Justification for SDG: The study focuses on the integration of equality and equity in transformative change, which directly relates to reducing inequalities in policy and research practices.
Keywords: equality, equity, transformative change, Transformative Innovation Policy, grounded theory

Title: The Sustainability of Urban Rooftop Farms
Abstract/Summary: This research focuses on urban rooftop farming initiatives as a solution to challenges posed by climate change and urbanization. The study aims to classify different types of rooftop farms and analyze their environmental and economic performance through life cycle assessment and costings. Findings indicate that the sustainability impact of rooftop farms varies widely based on their typology, farming methods, and functions. Current metrics for evaluating performance do not capture the full range of sustainability benefits, suggesting a need for new metrics to assess the overall impact of rooftop farms.
Specific Methods: Life Cycle Assessment, Life cycle costing
Flag: Missing Research Question, Missing SDG, Abstract too short

Title: Green hydrogen production and export potentials in the EU and neighbouring regions: Case studies from Georgia and Spain
Abstract/Summary: The growing ambition of climate targets within the European Union, necessary for maintaining global warming below 1.5 °C, have put green hydrogen (i.e. produced from renewable electricity) in the spotlight of decarbonisation pathways for sectors where replacing fossil fuels consumption is most challenging. Consequently, reliable assessments of potential supply and demand for this clean fuel are increasingly necessary for the European Union to pave its way forward. In this research a framework is developed to study countries’ possibilities to deploy large scale production, use and exports of green hydrogen. This framework combines technical calculations of demand and supply with economic cost assessments and a criteria approach that widens the scope of the analysis in order to encapsulate as many determining factors as possible for a successful hydrogen transition. The framework is then applied to the cases of Georgia and Spain.
Specific Methods: Energy Model, Cost calculations, Production, export calculations, Multi Criteria Analysis

Title: Finance for sustainability: How European actors can support sustainable small and medium-sized enterprises in Nigeria
Abstract/Summary: To address the challenge of mismanaged plastic waste in Nigeria, this research investigates the main research question ‘How can European actors financially support SMEs active in Nigeria’s plastic waste management as a means to aid in achieving the local sector’s sustainability transition?’. To answer this, a mixed-method approach was applied, including the review of the existing literature base, collecting insights from Nigerian SMEs through an online survey and conducting actor interviews with individuals working in SMEs, investors and further actors relevant to the topic. The findings reveal that SMEs are promising actors of change, and recommendations for financial instruments are provided to enhance their sustainability and support the informal sector.
Main Research Question: How can European actors financially support SMEs active in Nigeria’s plastic waste management as a means to aid in achieving the local sector’s sustainability transition?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on improving the sustainability of plastic waste management in Nigeria, which directly relates to responsible consumption and production practices.
Specific Methods: Literature review, Semi-structured interviews, Online questionnaire

Title: Stimulating climate adaptive community initiatives
Abstract/Summary: Community initiatives have an indispensable role to play in tackling climate adaptation, by their ability to increase the uptake of climate adaptation measures among households. This study focuses on increasing the uptake of green roofs among households specifically. Despite the potential of community initiatives in increasing this uptake, they often reach only a small portion of a neighborhood. By enhancing membership numbers of green roof community initiatives, the overall success of green roof implementation in the Netherlands may be enhanced. A novel framework of individual conditions has created a holistic overview of the relevant individual conditions that move people to participate in pro-environmental community initiatives. This study applies this framework to a case study in the Dutch city of Deventer, resulting in an understanding of which individual conditions are most important in driving people to join a climate adaptation community initiative.
Keywords: Citizen initiatives, nature-based solutions, neighborhood climate adaptation, framework of individual conditions, green roofs
Specific Methods: Case study, Online interviews, Survey interviews, Qualitative Interpretive Analysis, Qualitative Comparative Analysis

Title: Going circular in buildings’ renovations? An environmental and financial impact assessment conducted for a case study in Greece
Abstract/Summary: The current European building stock is responsible for 50 % of the energy use, 40 % of the greenhouse emissions, and 50 % of the raw material requirement. Existing European policies push the increase of renovations, aiming to achieve energy efficiency and material preservation. Applying circular strategies on upcoming renovations can boost this initiative. Until recently, existing literature has mainly been focused on monitoring the buildings’ operational energy and carbon emissions. In this study, a variety of environmental and financial implications was researched. The analysis was performed for a conventional and a circular renovation strategy applied to a residential building case study located in Athens, Greece. The impacts of the two strategies were accessed conducting a Material Flow Analysis (MFA) and a Decision Support System (DSS). The outcomes indicated that applying circularity in the Greek residential building stock is more beneficial from an environmental perspective, whilst from a financial aspect it turned out to be unattractive.
Keywords: Circular Economy, circular renovation, impact assessment, Material Flow Analysis, decision support system
Specific Methods: Material Flow Analysis, Decision Support System

Title: AGENT-BASED MODELLING OF THE HOUSE SPARROWS DESIGNING A SPATIAL BEHAVIOURAL MODEL FOR THE HOUSE SPARROW IN ZAANDAM
Abstract/Summary: The loss of biodiversity is a global problem, with a significant decline in house sparrows in north-western Europe, including the Netherlands. This research aims to explore the behavior and distribution of house sparrows using a conceptual behavioral model to predict their spatial distribution. An agent-based model was developed in Netlogo, resulting in various pathways for house sparrow distribution. Pathways 1 and 3 yielded the best results, while the standard pathway and pathway 4 performed poorly. The model shows that the suitability of houses greatly influences house sparrow distribution, although it does not fully align with real-world observations, indicating the need for further research.
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on the conservation of house sparrows, which are affected by biodiversity loss.

Title: Uncovering Landscapes of Vulnerability
Abstract/Summary: Indian coastal deltas, such as the Cauvery and Godavari Delta, consist of the world’s critical disaster risk hotspots. An important dimension of disaster risk is the societal conditions of vulnerability, consisting of the capacity to anticipate, cope with, resist, and recover from the impacts of a natural hazard. Vulnerability denotes a multidimensional concept encompassing many social, economic, political, and cultural conditions that differ through time and space. This research provides the first assessment of vulnerability in the Cauvery and Godavari Delta, as it aims to uncover the spatial and temporal dynamics of vulnerability in the two deltas. For this research, the Indian Delta Vulnerability Index was developed to measure and map the multidimensional level of vulnerability using secondary government data from 2011. Furthermore, a literature study and local expert consultations were used to better understand the spatial and temporal production of vulnerability. Three main conclusions emerge. First, a cross-delta vulnerability gradient was observed in both deltas, with the tail-end regions of the Cauvery and Godavari Deltas identified as significant vulnerability hotspots. Secondly, it was found that historical patterns of irrigation, agricultural productivity, marginalisation, and social differentiation shape the current vulnerability landscapes. Thirdly, the production of vulnerability was found to be shaped by complex spatial-temporal dynamics. Further comprehensive analysis across the two deltas is recommended to improve our understanding of the production and reproduction of the vulnerability landscapes.
Keywords: Vulnerability, Spatiality, Temporality, Cauvery, Godavari, Delta, India

Title: Facilitation for the future: The Ozone Secretariat’s role during the Kigali Amendment negotiations
Abstract/Summary: The Kigali Amendment is the latest addition to the Montreal Protocol for Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer. This Amendment ensures that HFCs are phased down under the Protocol’s regulations. The Ozone Secretariat has shown interest in phasing down HFCs directly after they were first discussed by some Parties. But why the Secretariat did so was puzzling. The research aims to answer why the Ozone Secretariat facilitated the Kigali Amendment negotiations. A hypothesis was built and tested through document analysis and qualitative interviews. The findings confirm the Secretariat had autonomy and influence, was eager to jump on HFCs, and their motivation was driven by a need for survival.
Main Research Question: Why did the Ozone Secretariat facilitate the Kigali Amendment negotiations?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the role of the Ozone Secretariat in climate change mitigation through the Kigali Amendment, which is crucial for reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
Keywords: Ozone Secretariat, Montreal Protocol, Kigali Amendment, HFCs, Survival, Problem-shifting

Title: History and belief coalitions in conservation conflict
Abstract/Summary: This research addresses human-wildlife conflict (HWC) in the Western Carpathians, where increasing human population and land development lead to conflicts over wolf protection and wild boar management. The study aims to explore how historical developments influence stakeholder cooperation in conservation. Data were collected through in-person interviews with 21 stakeholders, and network analysis and qualitative content analysis were employed. Findings indicate that policy core beliefs are crucial for coalition formation, and historical narratives bridge separate coalitions. The research highlights the importance of social factors in conservation conflicts and suggests that understanding these dynamics can foster cooperation.
Main Research Question: How do local historical developments influence stakeholder cooperation in the conservation and management of wolves and wild boar in the Western Carpathians?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on wildlife conservation and the management of species in their natural habitats, addressing the challenges posed by human-wildlife conflicts.
Keywords: conservation conflict, historical context, beliefs, coalitions, cooperation network
Specific Methods: In-person interviews, Network analysis, Qualitative content analysis

Title: Private Sector Involvement in Climate Adaptation
Abstract/Summary: Climate change poses an increasing risk to human and natural systems, and climate action is necessary to reduce these risks. The mobilisation of private sector investment and adaptation is needed but remains difficult. A strategy to encourage private sector engagement is through public-private partnerships. However, public-private partnerships are not always effective, and private sector engagement is sometimes still low in public-private partnerships. This research aims to investigate the role and level of engagement of the private sector, and its linked justice implications in public-private partnerships focused on food security adaptation in the global South. This research has conducted a case study analysis with four public-private partnerships concerned with agricultural adaptation in the global South. This thesis shows that the private sector often plays a key role within the public-private partnerships and are responsible for the actual delivery of the program or project.
Keywords: Climate Adaptation, Public-Private Partnerships, Justice, Private Sector Involvement, Food Security and Agriculture

Title: The impact of the photosynthetic traits’ downregulation on the leaf nutrient composition
Abstract/Summary: This research investigates the relationship between photosynthetic parameters and the nutrient composition of four plant species, focusing on nitrogen, phosphorus, and micronutrients such as zinc, iron, and calcium. The aim is to understand how atmospheric CO concentration affects these traits and nutrient content. A field study in the Ebro basin provided the plant material, and various statistical analyses were conducted to derive results. Findings indicate that increased CO leads to downregulation of photosynthetic traits, resulting in lower concentrations of leaf phosphorus and zinc. Further research is suggested to expand the analysis to other plant materials.
Main Research Question: What is the relationship between the photosynthetic parameters and the nutrient composition of selected plant species?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the impact of climate change on nutrient deficiencies, which is crucial for tackling global malnutrition.
Keywords: photosynthesis, nutrient composition, micronutrients, Climate Change, plant species

Title: Transmission Solutions for Floating Photovoltaics with Offshore Wind Energy in the North Sea - A Techno-Economic Analysis
Abstract/Summary: The transition towards renewable energy sources in the Netherlands has been driven by the need to meet future energy demands while addressing challenges such as limited land availability. This research conducts a techno-economic analysis to determine the optimal method for energy transmission from offshore floating photovoltaics integrated with offshore wind. The study identifies the 2 GW HVDC VSC transmission system as the best cost-efficient method for transporting energy. The integration of a hydrogen production plant can increase the capacity factor of the hybrid system, although it incurs additional costs. The findings suggest that exploring all available options is essential for enhancing energy security.
Main Research Question: What is the optimal method for energy transmission from offshore floating photovoltaics integrated with offshore wind?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the need for renewable energy solutions to meet future energy demands, contributing to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Specific Methods: Semi-structured interviews, Energy Model, Cost Modeling

Title: Justice in Soil Governance A Critical Discourse Analysis of Stakeholders’ Justice Perspectives Regarding the Proposed EU Soil Health Law
Abstract/Summary: Soils are of critical importance to human societies, as they provide a host of ecosystem services and harbor roughly a quarter to a third of all living things on Earth; soil degradation and biodiversity loss are pressing sustainability issues at both the global and the EU level. This thesis aims to provide a better understanding of stakeholders’ justice perspectives regarding EU soil health policy and how these perspectives may relate to stakeholder coalitions and policy proposals for the EU Soil Health Law proposed in 2022. The research follows principles of critical discourse analysis to analyze statements made by 31 stakeholders regarding EU soil policy. The results indicate that all justice perspectives included in the analysis can be identified in statements made by the stakeholders, with libertarianism and liberal egalitarianism being the most common. The findings illustrate the potential of an analysis of justice perspectives to identify and understand stakeholder coalitions and policy proposals.
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the thesis addresses soil degradation and biodiversity loss, which are critical issues for terrestrial ecosystems.
Theories: Pluralistic Justice Framework, Advocacy Coalition Framework

Title: Linking action and imagination: How climate court cases open up the public imagination and fuel inspiration
Abstract/Summary: The 2015 Paris Agreement has given rise to a new trend of climate litigation: strategic climate court cases. Distinct from traditional environmental litigation, these cases aim to bring about broader societal shifts, such as advancing effective climate policies and raising public awareness. This research studies climate court cases as a source of inspiration. A media analysis and 25 in-depth interviews with Dutch and Swiss climate activists revealed that these cases expand the horizons of what people can imagine, thereby opening up new ways of thinking and acting. The study underscores the central role of imagination in transformations, highlighting how it motivates action towards change.
Main Research Question: How do climate court cases influence public imagination and inspire action towards climate change?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the role of climate court cases in inspiring action and advancing climate policies, directly contributing to the goal of climate action.
Keywords: Imagination, imaginaries, emotional energy, climate action, strategic climate court cases

Title: Bridging knowledge between marine conservation actors in Pacific Small Island Developing States: A new perspective on the work of boundary organisations
Second reader: Prof. Dr. Peter P. J. Driessen
Abstract/Summary: In the face of growing pressures on the marine environment, the significance of evidence-based decision-making in the realm of marine conservation policies is of utmost importance. This thesis aims to improve knowledge uptake in Pacific Small Island Developing States (SIDS) by identifying factors and strategies for successful boundary work. Through interviews with boundary organisations, the study supplements existing literature on boundary organisations and develops a new framework for successful boundary work adapted to the realities in the Global South. The findings highlight the essential role of local communities in bridging the gap between marine science and policy, emphasizing the need for their engagement in the knowledge production and dissemination process.
Main Research Question: How can boundary organisations improve knowledge uptake in marine conservation policies in Pacific Small Island Developing States?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on marine conservation and the importance of evidence-based decision-making in marine policies.
Keywords: boundary organisations, marine conservation, knowledge exchange, Pacific SIDS, evidence-based decision-making

Title: Behavioural shifts of Diadema savignyi and their impact on coral communities around Rapa Nui
Abstract/Summary: This research investigates the behavioural shifts of the sea urchin Diadema savignyi and their impact on coral communities around Rapa Nui. The study aims to empirically determine these shifts and model their effects on coral and algae cover. Using a cellular automaton model, the research tests the influence of herbivory pressure on coral recruitment and cover. Findings indicate that while increased foraging rates and spatial aggregation of urchins were observed, they did not significantly alter model predictions. However, scenarios where urchins forage on coral recruits showed a decrease in coral cover with increased urchin density, suggesting that human activities may exacerbate the impact of urchin grazing on coral communities.
Main Research Question: How do sea urchin foraging behaviour vary with respect to the microhabitat in which they reside in the Rapa Nui coral communities?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on marine ecosystems, specifically the interactions between sea urchins and coral communities, which are crucial for maintaining biodiversity and ecosystem health.
Keywords: Diadema savignyi, coral communities, Rapa Nui, herbivory, ecosystem dynamics
Specific Methods: Empirical data analysis, Cellular automaton model simulations

Title: DEALING WITH SALINIZATION IN THE NETHERLANDS A GOOD GOVERNANCE CASE?
Abstract/Summary: Climate change is putting pressure on freshwater systems across the globe due to increased prevalence of more intense droughts, more extreme weather events and a rising sea-level. This research aimed to provide insights in characteristics of good salinization governance, evaluate the current Dutch salinization governance, and deliver recommendations for addressing bottlenecks within the governance system. An analytical framework was constructed for assessing the effectiveness, equitability, responsiveness, and robustness of the governance system. The results showed that policy documents and stakeholder opinions did not align, indicating a need for better implementation of policy to improve system performance.
Flag: Missing Research Question, Missing SDG, Abstract too short

Title: Assessing the potential for Negative Emission Technologies in Austria
Abstract/Summary: Negative Emission Technologies (NET) are critical for achieving the Paris Agreement goals to limit global warming. This thesis assesses the techno-economic and implementation potential of various NETs in Austria, including Afforestation & Reforestation, Biochar, and Direct Air Carbon Capture. The results indicate a technical potential for negative emissions of 39 Mt CO2 by 2050, with 30 Mt CO2 realizable cost-effectively at a carbon price of 100€ per tonne. The findings can guide policymakers in selecting appropriate NET portfolios for implementation in Austria.
Main Research Question: What is the techno-economic and implementation potential for Negative Emission Technologies in Austria?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the urgent need for climate action by assessing the potential of Negative Emission Technologies to help Austria achieve its climate neutrality goals.
Keywords: Negative Emission Technologies, Austria, climate neutrality, carbon capture, afforestation
Specific Methods: Literature review, Expert interviews, quantitative potential assessment, Scenario analysis, Multi-Criteria Analysis

Title: Can the share be fair? A study on the cost-benefit analysis of the redistribution of mitigation costs burden sharing regimes
Abstract/Summary: Mitigation of climate change is the biggest challenge of the 21st century. The Paris Agreement set 2ºC as the threshold not to cross. Integrated Assessment Models have made estimations of the costs associated with climate change as well as those of mitigation. This research examined the effects of various burden sharing regimes on the distribution of mitigation costs and allowances. By looking at GDP per capita, historical emissions, and population shares, this study explores different approaches to fairness. The results indicate that principles of fairness need to be agreed upon by the international community to implement effective mechanisms for emission trading and financing of mitigation.
Main Research Question: How can the allocation of estimated costs for climate change mitigation be distributed among regions to ensure an equitable sharing of burdens, whilst preserving economic benefits?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the equitable distribution of climate change mitigation costs, which is essential for achieving global climate action goals.

Title: Fostering effective sustainability governance through standards - a comparative analysis of B Corp and ISO14001 certification schemes
Abstract/Summary: Sustainability Certification Schemes (SCS) have evolved as a transformative form of social and environmental governance, particularly among private companies. This research aims to assist in navigating desirable SCS to advance sustainable development by understanding which SCS are more effective in fostering sustainability governance. An extensive literature review was conducted to create a theoretical framework for evaluating effective governance conditions. The framework was applied to B Corp and ISO 14001:2015 standards. Findings indicate both standards perform well in terms of effectiveness, but shortcomings exist in addressing stakeholder cost distribution. Future research should explore the complexity of interactions between governance conditions.
Main Research Question: What conditions make sustainability certification schemes more effective in fostering sustainability governance among companies?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on sustainability certification schemes that aim to improve corporate sustainability practices and governance.
Keywords: Effective Institutional Governance, Sustainable Development, Conditions for Effective Governance, Good-governance Principles, Sustainability Standards, Certification Schemes

Title: Active Rewilding in a Novel Ecosystem: Investigating the Impact of Deer Herbivory and Water Level Management on Wetland Vegetation Development
Supervisor: Dr. Ralph J.M. Temmink, Kerstin Bouma
Abstract/Summary: Wetlands play a crucial role in providing ecosystem services that contribute to the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). However, human activities have led to widespread degradation of wetlands over the past three centuries, increasing efforts for wetland restoration. This study investigated the effects of red deer herbivory and artificial water level management on vegetation development during the early stage of ecological succession in Oostvaardersplassen park. Findings demonstrated that deer herbivory adversely affected reed presence and coverage, while herbivory exclusion increased vegetation coverage. Water level fluctuations also influenced vegetation communities. The insights gained are valuable for shaping wetland restoration strategies, contributing to the preservation of wetland ecosystem services.
Main Research Question: What are the effects of red deer herbivory and artificial water level management on vegetation development in Oostvaardersplassen park?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on wetland restoration and the impact of herbivory on vegetation, which are critical for maintaining terrestrial ecosystems and biodiversity.
Keywords: rewilding, herbivory, wetland restoration, vegetation development, Oostvaardersplassen

Title: Estimating problem shifting from the Minamata Convention on Mercury
Abstract/Summary: The challenge of global environmental governance lies largely in implementing effective solutions to environmental problems as articulated in multilateral environmental agreements (MEAs). This study assesses the Minamata Convention, an MEA tasked with managing anthropogenic mercury emissions, to evaluate problem shifting caused by its implementation. By analyzing over 1100 implementation measures from national reports, the study identifies eight measures that generate new problems, particularly affecting lower-income populations. The findings highlight the need for preventative measures within MEAs to mitigate problem shifting and suggest that the methodology can be applied to other agreements.
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the health implications of mercury management and the unintended consequences of policy measures that affect human health, particularly for vulnerable populations.
Flag: Missing Research Question, Abstract too short

Title: Sexuality education as a weapon against gendered oppression? A case study of the Amazing You! sexuality education programme and its effects on children in Malindi, Kenya.
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the Amazing You! sexuality education programme in Malindi, Kenya, aiming to understand its impact on children and its role in combating gendered oppression. The research employs qualitative methods, including interviews and focus group discussions, to gather insights from participants and stakeholders. Findings indicate that the programme fosters protective behaviors and promotes gender equality, yet challenges remain due to cultural beliefs and societal norms that perpetuate gendered oppression.
Main Research Question: How does the Amazing You! sexuality education programme affect children in Malindi, Kenya, in the context of gendered oppression?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses gendered oppression and the role of education in promoting gender equality, aligning with the goal of empowering all women and girls.
Keywords: Sexuality education, gendered oppression, Amazing You!, Malindi, Kenya
Theories: Feminist Theory, Theory of Planned Behaviour

Title: Developing a Conceptual Framework For The Comprehensiveness of Social Life Cycle Assessment - A Case Study on Oiconomy Pricing
Abstract/Summary: Achieving sustainability in global value chains is a complex challenge for businesses pursuing the 2030 UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). This research seeks to improve our understanding of social life cycle assessment (S-LCA) methodology comprehensiveness by creating a framework based on scientific discussions and applying it to a case study on Oiconomy Pricing. The study examines whether Oiconomy Pricing's design and social impact categories can universally evaluate social life cycle impacts. The main research question is how comprehensive the Oiconomy System is as an S-LCA instrument. The findings indicate that Oiconomy Pricing is a comprehensive methodology by design but neglected to include diverse stakeholder interests associated with social capital.
Main Research Question: How comprehensive is the Oiconomy System as an S-LCA instrument?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the need for improved sustainability practices in global value chains, aligning with the goal of responsible consumption and production.
Keywords: Social Life Cycle Assessment, Oiconomy Pricing, Sustainability, comprehensive methodology, stakeholder inclusiveness
Specific Methods: Literature review, Interviews, materiality analysis, Expert interviews

Title: How to be a Permie? Analyzing Influential Factors Shaping Permaculture Strategies for Overcoming Challenges in Southern Australia.
Abstract/Summary: Permaculture, as an alternative and sustainable approach to conventional farming, has been an emerging field of study, primarily focusing on challenges faced and strategies applied during the initiation phase of permaculture enterprises. This research seeks to bridge a critical gap in the existing literature by exploring the factors that shape the strategies employed by long-term successful permaculture practitioners in southern Australia. Through an explorative case study, this research uncovers unique revelations that shed new light on permaculture practices, highlighting the importance of individual-level factors such as motivation and personal values, as well as social-level factors like shared values and community support. The study provides insights into the potential of permaculture practitioners and offers promising prospects for future research.

Title: Cascading problems through the Ramsar Convention
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the cascading problems associated with the Ramsar Convention, focusing on the governance challenges it faces. The aim is to analyze how decision-making processes within the Convention contribute to problem shifting among multiple environmental agreements. A qualitative empirical research methodology is employed, utilizing case studies and data analysis to uncover the dynamics of these cascades. The findings reveal significant gaps in data sharing and compliance among contracting parties, highlighting the need for improved coordination and accountability.
Main Research Question: How do decision-making processes within the Ramsar Convention contribute to cascading problems in environmental governance?

Title: CHANGING LANDSCAPES AND RURAL LIVELIHOODS: THE CASE OF GERMİYAN NEIGHBORHOOD IN ÇEŞME DISTRICT, TURKEY EXPLORING RURAL COMMUNITIES’ RESPONSES TO LARGE-SCALE LAND INVESTMENTS
Abstract/Summary: This research investigates the impacts of large-scale land investments on land use and land cover change (LULCC) and the livelihood strategies of the rural community in the Çeşme District of Turkey. The aim is to explore how these investments affect local communities and their responses. Using an exploratory case study approach, the study includes interviews with local authorities and a survey of the rural community. Findings indicate that the majority of the community perceives negative impacts, including loss of pasturelands, environmental pollution, and inadequate support from authorities. The primary strategy employed by the community against these impacts is legal action, highlighting the need for proactive measures to protect their rights.
Main Research Question: How do large-scale land investments impact land use and land cover change and the livelihood strategies of the rural community in the Çeşme District?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the challenges posed by large-scale land investments on rural communities, emphasizing the need for sustainable urban and rural development practices.
Keywords: large-scale investments, land use change, land cover change, rural livelihoods, development, rural community, Sustainability
Theories: Environmental Justice, Sustainable Livelihoods Framework, Common-Pool Resources

Title: The impact of paludiculture on farmers’ livelihoods
Abstract/Summary: This research addresses the impact of paludiculture on farmers’ livelihoods, focusing on case studies in Sweden and Kalimantan, Indonesia. The aim is to examine how this sustainable production method affects social and economic aspects of farming. Methodology includes a literature review and in-depth semi-structured interviews with farmers and experts. Findings indicate that paludiculture can improve food security and reduce environmental risks, but also leads to economic inequality and dependency among farmers. The study highlights the need for inclusive practices to ensure equitable benefits from paludiculture.
Main Research Question: What is the impact of paludiculture on farmers’ livelihoods?
Justification for SDG: Paludiculture contributes to the restoration of peatlands, which is essential for biodiversity and ecosystem health, aligning with the goal of protecting, restoring, and promoting sustainable use of terrestrial ecosystems.
Keywords: Paludiculture, peatlands, nature-based solutions, sustainable agriculture, food security.

Title: Gender Transformative Approaches – a silver bullet for gender equality? Investigating the effects of the GALS methodology on gender transformative change for coffee smallholders, as implemented in the Circular Coffee Project in San Martín, Peru
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the effects of the Gender Action Learning System (GALS) methodology on gender transformative change among coffee smallholders in San Martín, Peru. The aim is to understand how GALS contributes to gender equality by addressing social norms and power structures. Qualitative field research was conducted through workshops, interviews, and participant observation. Findings indicate that GALS promotes recognition of women's work and more equitable resource sharing, but institutional barriers hinder progress. The study concludes that a critical, intersectional approach is necessary for effective gender transformative change in agricultural contexts.
Main Research Question: In what ways does the GALS approach contribute to gender transformative change within coffee smallholder’s livelihoods?
Justification for SDG: The study focuses on gender transformative change and the empowerment of women in agricultural supply chains, directly addressing the goal of achieving gender equality.
Keywords: Gender Inequality, Gender Transformative Approach (GTA), Gender Transformative Change (GTC), Intersectionality, Livelihoods, Smallholders, Coffee Production, Peru
Specific Methods: Evaluation workshops, Semi-structured interviews, Visual elicitation, Participant observation, Policy analysis

Title: Stakeholder Frames of Carbon Farming in the German Agricultural Sector Analysing Cooperative and Conflictual Dynamics
Abstract/Summary: Soil carbon sequestration is claimed to offer a potential solution to climate change and unsustainable agricultural practices by transferring CO2 from the atmosphere into the soil. This study delves into the complex world of carbon farming and its adoption in the German agricultural sector, using frame theory to explore the diverse perspectives of stakeholders. This mixed methods study integrates stakeholder analysis, content analysis, and semi-structured interviews, offering valuable insights into the multifaceted landscape of carbon farming in Germany. The findings contribute to a more comprehensive understanding of the issues at hand and pave the way for more informed and effective decision-making.
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the role of carbon farming in mitigating climate change, highlighting its potential to enhance resilience and improve soil health.
Keywords: Carbon farming, frame analysis, stakeholder dynamics, Germany
Specific Methods: Stakeholder analysis, Content analysis, Semi-structured interviews

Title: Assessing Solar PV System Performance at Utrecht Science Park
Abstract/Summary: This research examines the performance of the Utrecht University Science Park (USP) solar PV system over a five-year period (2018-2022). USP system's energy output consistently surpassed the target of 1 million kWh annually, reaffirming its effectiveness in sustainable energy generation. KNN imputation was used to address data gaps, enhancing analysis accuracy. Assessment of performance metrics, encompassing energy output, final yield, reference yield, and performance ratio, illuminated system efficiency and variability. South-oriented, 30-degree tilt subsystems outperformed, while W-E-S orientation yielded the least. Total in-plane irradiation yield was modeled with Python pvlib and Solargis Prospect Model, with the former generated higher reference yields due to its clear sky modeling, while Solargis introduced more nuanced predictions based on historical weather patterns.

Title: Measuring maturity in corporate Circular Business Models
Abstract/Summary: This study addresses the underdevelopment of the circular economy (CE) as an alternative to the linear economy. The aim is to develop a maturity model for Circular Business Model (CBM) implementation, providing a diagnostic and benchmarking tool for organizations and policymakers. The research follows a deductive design, conducting an integrative literature review to identify 14 organizational capabilities for CBM implementation. The model is tested with two case studies from the denim industry, refining it based on feedback. The developed maturity model offers guidance for organizations to evaluate their CBM implementation status and plan future steps.
Main Research Question: How can organizations measure their maturity in terms of CBM implementation?
Justification for SDG: The study promotes the implementation of circular business models, which contribute to sustainable consumption and production patterns.
Keywords: CBM implementation, maturity models, dynamic capabilities, Circular Economy, corporate capabilities

Title: The Equivalence of Injustice
Abstract/Summary: Ten years after the first gas-mining earthquake in Groningen, the people of the region still suffer regular quakes and the effects of the poor political response. This research examines the functioning of the environmental justice framework in describing the lived experiences of injustice in the Netherlands by comparing the experiences of the people of Groningen to those of the Niger Delta. The qualitative research involved meetings with organizations, focus groups, and interviews with inhabitants of both regions. The findings indicate that the frameworks can capture the lived experiences in Groningen and reveal similarities in experiences despite differing impacts of injustice.
Main Research Question: How does the environmental justice framework describe the lived experiences of injustice in Groningen compared to those in the Niger Delta?
Justification for SDG: This research highlights the disparities in experiences of injustice between privileged and marginalized communities, emphasizing the need for equitable treatment and recognition of all affected populations.

Title: Surplus Food Redistribution: Towards a World Where No Good Food Goes to Waste
Abstract/Summary: Food waste is a major global issue, with negative environmental, social and economic impacts. Surplus food redistribution is a strategy, through which the problem of food waste can be effectively combatted by redirecting edible food, which would otherwise be wasted, to people. In the European Union, 127 kg/capita of food is wasted every year, while 36.2 million people cannot afford a nutritious meal every second day. Reducing these figures is paramount, and can be achieved effectively through surplus food redistribution. The aim of this research was to explore surplus food redistribution possibilities across the Member States of the European Union, present an overview of practices from the non-profit and for-profit sectors, and find best practice examples through a multi-criteria decision analysis. This research found that food waste can be combatted through surplus food redistribution in the EU with increased funding and awareness of surplus food redistribution organisations and their activities.
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on reducing food waste through surplus food redistribution, addressing both environmental and social issues related to food security.
Organizations Studied: Fédération Belge des Banques Alimentaires, Česká Federace Potravinových Bank, Toidupank, Fédération Française des Banques Alimentaires, Magyar Élelmiszerbank Egyesület, FoodCloud, Fondazione Banco Alimentare ONLUS, Maisto Bankas, Federacja Polskich Banków Żywności, Federación Española de Bancos de Alimentos

Title: The Transformative Potential of Dutch Community Supported Agriculture
Abstract/Summary: This research investigates the potential of Dutch community supported agriculture (CSA) to transform the Dutch food system, which faces various ecological and socio-economic challenges. The study aims to map CSA's transformative potential and explore ways to enhance it. Through an extensive literature review and semi-structured interviews with CSA farmers, members, and experts, the findings reveal that CSA can challenge conventional food system philosophies and foster social communities that reconnect society and nature. The research identifies enablers and barriers for CSA's transformative potential across practical, political, and deeper layers of values and beliefs, suggesting that holistic education and supportive governance can facilitate CSA's emergence.
Main Research Question: How can Dutch community supported agriculture (CSA) contribute to the structural transformation of the Dutch food system?
Justification for SDG: The research highlights the need for sustainable practices in the food system, which aligns with the goal of ensuring sustainable consumption and production patterns.
Keywords: Community Supported Agriculture, Sustainable Food Systems, Transformation, Enablers, Barriers
Theories: Multi-Level Perspective, Three Spheres of Transformation

Title: Understanding the challenges for citizens in the municipal heat transition: A Case study of the city of Itzehoe, Germany
Abstract/Summary: This research addresses the challenges for citizens' involvement in the municipal heat transition in Itzehoe, Germany, amidst the ongoing energy crisis. The study aims to understand these challenges through a case study involving survey research, interviews with local key actors, and policy analysis. Findings reveal that while citizens are aware of the energy transition's importance and are motivated to contribute, structural challenges hinder their involvement. A clear vision for the transition process and improved information flow from various stakeholders to citizens are essential for enhancing participation.
Main Research Question: What are the challenges for citizens' involvement in the heat transition in Itzehoe, Germany?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on the energy transition and the role of citizens in achieving a sustainable energy system, directly aligning with the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: heat transition, energy citizenship, energy justice, municipal energy policy, citizen involvement
Specific Methods: Survey, Policy analysis, Interviews with local key actors

Title: Evaluating forest restoration effects on timing of avian dawn chorus in Ranomafana National Park, Madagascar
Abstract/Summary: Monitoring of forest restoration efforts is essential to ensure healthy, self-sustaining tropical rainforests. Passive acoustic monitoring is used to monitor vocal activity of birds, which play a key role in forest ecosystems as seed dispersers. Communication between birds seems most profitable during a peak of bird singing in the morning, known as the dawn chorus. Anthropogenic disturbances leading to increased light levels affect the timing of this chorus in individual species. This research sheds a light on the effect of forest restoration on the dawn chorus using automatic detection methods to identify bird sounds from acoustic data. Machine learning methods like clustering and pattern matching were used alongside a manual analysis to describe the dawn chorus in protected forests as well as restoration sites around Ranomafana National Park, Madagascar.
Keywords: Acoustic monitoring, forest restoration, dawn chorus, avian singing, bird monitoring, automatic recognition
Specific Methods: Passive acoustic monitoring, Machine learning methods, Clustering, Pattern matching, Manual analysis

Title: Operationalising intergenerational justice for long-term geological storage and disposal
Abstract/Summary: Intergenerational justice concerns itself with the just distribution of risks and benefits across generations, including people thousands of years into the future, and is inherent in the concept of sustainability. This research aims to develop an assessment framework for how intergenerational justice is operationalised in long-term policy, applied to nuclear waste disposal and carbon capture and storage (CCS) policies in the Netherlands, UK, Finland, and Norway. The framework consists of ten criteria based on six principles. Findings indicate that nuclear waste disposal policies perform better than CCS policies, but both need improvements to adequately safeguard intergenerational justice.
Main Research Question: How should intergenerational justice be operationalised in long-term policy to safeguard the rights of future generations?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the long-term impacts of nuclear waste disposal and carbon capture and storage, which are critical for mitigating climate change and ensuring sustainability for future generations.

Title: HUMAN BEHAVIOUR, EMOTIONS, AND PERCEPTIONS DURING HUMAN-WILDLIFE INTERACTIONS IN THE NETHERLANDS: A MOBILE APP BASED APPROACH
Abstract/Summary: Human-wildlife interactions (HWIs) are increasingly prevalent in the Netherlands due to urbanisation, climate change, and habitat conversion. This study aims to explore the drivers of human behaviour, emotions, and perceptions during HWIs using a mobile application prototype developed for citizen scientists. Data was collected over six weeks in two natural areas, employing statistical tests to analyze the influence of animal species on human responses. Key findings indicate that interactions are predominantly positive, with significant effects of animal species on human behaviour and perceptions. However, no significant relationship was found between human emotions, perceptions, and behaviours, suggesting a need for qualitative analysis. This research offers a novel approach for future studies on HWIs.
Main Research Question: What is the influence of animal species on human behaviour, emotions, and perceptions during human-wildlife interactions?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on the coexistence of humans and wildlife, addressing the impacts of wildlife conservation and management on human perceptions and behaviours.
Keywords: Human-Wildlife Interactions, Citizen Science, Mobile Application, Emotion, Behaviour
Specific Methods: Case study, Pearson’s chi-squared, Fisher’s exact tests, Correlation analysis, multiple ordinal regression, Qualitative analysis

Title: A proof of concept for mapping environmental change in areas of resource extraction at the source of energy transition supply chains.
Abstract/Summary: The availability of cloud computing and open access satellite data provides new opportunities for the application of earth observation (EO) based environmental monitoring. Mapping the environmental change resulting from resource extraction at the source of energy transition supply chains represents a particularly interesting application for these services. Using Google Earth Engine, Landsat and Sentinel imagery, I developed an example of a workflow model to create remote sensing time series to measure land cover and vegetation change. The model was applied to two case studies in Cabo Delgado, Mozambique, showing significant increases in unvegetated areas due to extraction activities. The workflow model yielded high classification accuracies and can be beneficial for monitoring change in vulnerable regions.
Main Research Question: How can remote sensing time series be used to map environmental change in areas of resource extraction at the source of energy transition supply chains?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on monitoring environmental changes due to resource extraction, which directly impacts land and ecosystems.
Keywords: remote sensing, Energy transition, time series, open access, resource extraction

Title: Do-it-yourself greening: Interaction between municipal authorities and community-led initiatives for urban green spaces
Abstract/Summary: The effects of urbanisation and climate change have increased the focus of urban policymakers on sustainability. Nature-Based Solutions (NBS) are increasingly implemented as a means to achieve sustainability, through their versatility and provision of services to urban inhabitants. This thesis examines how municipalities enable and constrain urban green space community-led initiatives (CLI) through their interaction. Data was collected from the Dutch municipalities of Culemborg and Wageningen using a case study approach with interviews and documentation. The research found that municipalities and green space CLIs interact through the reflexive provision of resources and rules, with municipal employees acting as boundary spanners. Reflexivity is a key dimension for interaction, smoothing over obstacles within the policy landscape.
Main Research Question: How, why, and with what implications do municipalities enable and constrain urban green space CLIs through their interaction?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on enhancing urban green spaces through community-led initiatives, contributing to sustainable urban development.

Title: A temporal perspective on area-oriented processes in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: This research addresses the temporal challenge of reconciling short-term and long-term objectives in area-oriented processes, particularly in the context of the water and land system as a steering concept. The study identifies five temporal strategies and evaluates their application through case studies involving water managers. Findings indicate that these strategies can provide critical insights for integrating short-term actions with long-term planning, although further conceptual development is needed.
Main Research Question: How can temporal action perspectives be identified and utilized by water managers to reconcile short-term and long-term objectives in area-oriented processes?
Keywords: area-oriented processes, temporal strategies, the water and land system as a steering concept, water managers

Title: Participation in International Cooperative Initiatives (ICIs) – Incentivising and Enhancing Local Non-State Actors in Sustainable Transitions in the Global South
Abstract/Summary: This research examines the role of International Cooperative Initiatives (ICIs) in enhancing local non-state actors' participation in sustainable transitions, particularly in the Global South. The study aims to explore the practices and mechanisms that ICIs implement to incentivise collective climate action. A mixed-methods approach, including a large-N survey and case studies, is employed to analyze the engagement of ICIs with local actors and the benefits perceived by participants. Findings reveal the structural elements that contribute to the success of ICIs and highlight the opportunities and challenges faced by local actors in these international networks.
Main Research Question: How do International Cooperative Initiatives (ICIs) incentivise and enhance local non-state actors' participation in sustainable transitions?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on enhancing climate action through the engagement of local non-state actors in sustainable transitions, addressing the urgent need for collective climate action in the face of climate change.
Keywords: International Cooperative Initiatives, incentive and enhancement mechanisms, sustainable transitions, collective action
Specific Methods: Large-N survey, Desk research, Case study, Semi-structured interviews
Organizations Studied: Global Forum on Agricultural Research & Innovation, Health Care Without Harm Southeast Asia, PROAmazonía

Title: Governance Capacity in Addressing Urban Green (In)Justice: A Comparative Study of Rotterdam and Utrecht
Abstract/Summary: This research focuses on the governance capacity of Rotterdam and Utrecht in addressing urban green (in)justice. With the vulnerability of urban areas to climate change impacts and the increasing need for cities to take action, urban greening is being utilized as a strategy to adapt to climate change. However, recent studies indicate that the benefits of urban greening are unevenly distributed, leading to environmental (in)justice issues. This research employs an embedded multiple case study methodology with the aim to enhance understanding of how urban green (in)justice is assessed and analyze the governance capacity in relation to urban green (in)justice. The findings provide valuable insights for Rotterdam, Utrecht, and other cities, facilitating the development of more inclusive and equitable approaches to urban greening in the face of climate change challenges.
Main Research Question: How do Rotterdam and Utrecht assess and address urban green (in)justice through their governance capacity?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research addresses urban green (in)justice, which is crucial for creating sustainable and equitable urban environments.
Keywords: urban green (in)justice, governance capacity, Climate Change, Rotterdam, Utrecht

Title: The Capabilities Sprint Workshop
Abstract/Summary: The Circular Economy is seen by many governments and businesses as key to promoting sustainability. Value chains are also integral to the success of the Circular Economy transition. While there are tools that businesses can use to strategize their approach towards a circular business model, there is a gap in tools that address transitioning itself for value chain actors. Therefore, this thesis’s main research aim was to propose how value chains can improve their ability to collectively transitions towards a more sustainable and circular configuration by developing and validating a tool prototype. To achieve this, first, a brief non-systematic literature review of impact assessment, followed by a brief structured literature review on conditions for transitioning, was executed. This was done to identify relevant inputs for the tool. Then, tool development was driven through feedback sessions with TNO employees. Lastly, the final tool prototype was evaluated in a final feedback round with TNO employees.
Main Research Question: How can value chains improve their ability to collectively transition towards a more sustainable and circular configuration?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the thesis focuses on developing a tool to enhance sustainability within value chains, promoting circular business models.
Specific Methods: Literature review, Tool Design, Feedback Sessions

Title: The potential of diversification in coffee systems to enhance coffee productivity, biological pest control services and pollination services: a systematic review
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the impact of plant diversity in coffee systems on productivity, biological pest control, and pollination services. The aim is to understand the interactions between these factors and the potential trade-offs involved. A systematic review of 77 studies was conducted, and linear mixed-effect models were used to analyze the data. Findings reveal strong positive correlations between biodiversity-mediated ecosystem services and coffee productivity, while a trade-off exists between coffee productivity and biological pest control services. The results suggest that increasing plant diversity can enhance ecosystem services but may negatively impact coffee productivity.
Main Research Question: What is the direct impact of plant diversity on coffee productivity, and indirectly through biological pest control and pollination services?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the understanding of biodiversity conservation in agricultural systems, which is essential for achieving sustainable land management and ecosystem health.
Keywords: agroforestry, coffee, pollination services, pest control services, productivity
Specific Methods: Systematic literature review, Linear mixed-effect models

Title: Collaborative Governance for Adaptation to Multi-Risk in the Dutch North Sea
Abstract/Summary: The Dutch North Sea is a space of tremendous economic potential in which efficient organisation of space is key for enduring climate adaptation. Climate change exacerbates natural hazards that pose dangers to the infrastructure set out to be installed at sea. The Dutch Government elaborated a medium-term plan that steers the system towards key transitions in energy production, food systems and ecosystem conservation. The user groups are currently participating in the governance efforts but there are still trade-offs to be made. Dynamic Adaptive Policy Pathways for Multi Risk (DAPP-MR) represents a methodology in development that MYRIAD-EU aims to tailor for use in transboundary sea settings. This research analyses the collaborative governance regime of the Dutch North Sea to understand where potential lock-ins might occur when attempting to implement DAPP-MR in this system. Taking advantage of being part of the MYRIAD-EU project and the collaboration with Deltares, this research uses primary data from the first North Sea pilot stakeholder workshop and internal sessions. Concurrently, interviews with experts in the field were conducted and an extensive literature review and document analysis were also used. The results showed a governance system that has already started to implement collaborative processes, but the system also has a preference for some activities over others. The user groups that fell within the spectrum of the research have displayed siloed thinking when it comes to climate adaptation. Both compatibilities and incompatibilities between activities would impact how multi-use of space can occur. There are knowledge gaps regarding systems impacts, and those are being taken into decision-making due to the urgency with which the government treats climate action. Furthermore, there were also gaps in single hazards to multi-hazard to multi-risk thinking in this system. DAPP-MR could be used to navigate the uncertainties of this system in theory, but more research and potential changes to the method are required. The conceptualisation of space as the resource of a socio-ecological system represents a starting point in both developing collaborative governance and DAPP-MR for use in maritime areas. Further research is required in both fields, as well as in multi-hazard and multi-risk thinking that should develop in this area.
Main Research Question: What are the potential lock-ins of the collaborative governance regime in developing adaptation pathways in the Dutch North Sea?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the urgent need for adaptation strategies in response to climate change impacts on the Dutch North Sea, highlighting the importance of collaborative governance in managing multi-risks.
Keywords: North Sea, adaptation pathways, multi-risk, collaborative governance
Specific Methods: Workshop, Semi-structured interviews, Document analysis, Literature review
Theories: Collaborative Governance, Dynamic Adaptive Policy Pathways

Title: Water and Ground Governance on Multiple Levels: an ex-ante assessment of the Netherlands’s new ‘Water and Ground Steering’ policy framework
Abstract/Summary: Amid the increasing severity of floods and dwindling land availability, the management and governance of water and ground have moved up the social and political agendas. This research revolves around an ex-ante assessment of the ‘Water and Ground steering’ policy framework in the Netherlands. The aim is to evaluate the effectiveness of multi-level governance (MLG) policies. A novel analytical framework was developed, and an embedded case study analysis was conducted, including interviews with policy advisors. The findings indicate that the effectiveness of the policy can be assessed and highlight governance strategies that can enhance this effectiveness.
Main Research Question: What governance strategies are able to enhance the effectiveness of a MLG policy, and to what extent?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on water governance and management, which directly relates to ensuring availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all.
Keywords: Multi-Level Governance, Water management, Policy Effectiveness, Governance Strategies

Title: Women’s Work: Making Sense of Energy Poverty through Gendered Social Practices in Kanaleneiland, Utrecht
Abstract/Summary: In 2022, over half of Dutch households have difficulties paying their general expenses and 602,000 households are estimated to live in energy poverty. This qualitative study uses a social practice approach to explore how energy poverty is understood and acted upon through gendered energy practices in Kanaleneiland, Utrecht. Ethnographic fieldwork, (in)formal interviews, and participatory action research were employed. Findings indicate that women are disproportionately affected by energy poverty due to cultural and gendered dimensions, with low-technical solutions associated with femininity. The study highlights the importance of recognizing women's experiences and the structural inequalities underlying energy poverty to ensure a more inclusive energy transition.
Main Research Question: How is energy poverty understood and acted upon through gendered energy practices in Kanaleneiland, Utrecht?
Justification for SDG: This study addresses the gendered dimensions of energy poverty, highlighting how women are more vulnerable and the need for gender-sensitive approaches in energy policy.
Keywords: energy poverty, gender, social practices, Kanaleneiland, Utrecht

Title: Realising Transformative Change through the Evaluation of Urban Nature-Based Solutions: An Exploration of the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: The dual crises of climate change and biodiversity loss increase the vulnerability of cities, which are dealing with challenges of extreme weather events, degrading urban ecosystems and increasing temperatures. Nature-based solutions (NBS) can contribute to tackling these challenges whilst simultaneously improving the health and well-being of urban residents. This research aims to explore how evaluations of urban NBS currently capture the aspired multiple benefits of NBS, their possible trade-offs and issues of justice and inclusivity. The research developed six evaluation principles and is based on a case study analysis of 42 evaluation documents of Dutch urban NBS, supplemented by 10 interviews. The findings reveal that most evaluations align with the principles, but many do not adequately address justice and inclusivity.
Main Research Question: How do evaluations of urban nature-based solutions currently capture the aspired multiple benefits, trade-offs, and issues of justice and inclusivity, and how does this relate to their potential for transformative change?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on urban nature-based solutions that aim to enhance urban resilience and sustainability, addressing the challenges posed by climate change and biodiversity loss in cities.

Title: EXPLORING THE FINANCING OF NATURE-BASED SOLUTIONS BY TRANSNATIONAL GOVERNANCE INITIATIVES
Abstract/Summary: This research examines the financing of Nature-Based Solutions (NBS) through transnational governance initiatives (TGIs) in the context of climate change and biodiversity loss. The aim is to explore how TGIs influence funding flows for NBS that address both climate and biodiversity objectives. A mixed methods research design is employed, combining quantitative data analysis with qualitative case studies. The findings reveal that TGIs facilitate funding from the Global North to the Global South, primarily using traditional financial instruments, while also enabling non-state actors to influence funding flows. The study highlights the potential of TGIs to provide joint climate-biodiversity finance, but also raises concerns about reinforcing existing power imbalances.
Main Research Question: How do transnational governance initiatives shape the flow of finance for climate related nature-based solutions?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the urgent need for funding mechanisms that support climate-related interventions, particularly those that integrate biodiversity objectives, aligning with the goals of SDG 13.
Keywords: Transnational Governance, Climate Finance, Biodiversity Finance, Nature-Based Solutions

Title: Experiencing Nature in Food Forests in Utrecht
Abstract/Summary: Food forests can serve many functions, one of which is addressing nature estrangement in (semi)urban environments. The problem of nature estrangement is not so much caused by technology, urbanization, or even having less contact with nature, but by over-rationalisation, objectivization, suppressed emotions, and anthropocentrism. To reshape our relation to nature we must first change the way we think about and perceive it. Therefore, I sought to answer the following question: in what ways can experiences of nature in food forests in Utrecht provide opportunities for creating connection, affect, and emotional attachment with the more than human world? To answer the research questions, I first conducted a literature review to explore the various theories and debates about the influence of environmental aesthetics on the perception people have of nature and their encounters with the natural environment. Lastly, I conducted three focus groups to share my insights and engage participants in emotionally meaningful encounters with their environment.
Main Research Question: In what ways can experiences of nature in food forests in Utrecht provide opportunities for creating connection, affect, and emotional attachment with the more than human world?
Keywords: Human-nature relationships, environmental aesthetics, food forests, imagination
Theories: Environmental aesthetics, Imagination, Compassion, Practical Wisdom

Title: Justly Closing the Loop in South Africa
Abstract/Summary: In the light of climate change, there is a need to shift away from South Africa’s resource intensive economy. Recently, more focus on making resource use more sustainable has been put to a circular economy; however the social dimension has not fully been explored in the context of transitioning to a circular economy. This research looks at aspects that must be considered in a just transition to a circular economy through a literature review, workshops, and interviews with stakeholders across the country. Key aspects identified include the country’s Apartheid history, access to services, governance, and psychological factors. Recommendations for specific projects to tackle these challenges were made, emphasizing the need for tailored solutions to local contexts.
Main Research Question: What aspects must be considered in a just transition to a circular economy in South Africa?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on transitioning to a circular economy, which aims to promote sustainable resource use and address social inequalities in South Africa.
Keywords: Circular Economy, Just Transition, South Africa, inequalities, stakeholder engagement
Theories: Doughnut Economics, Planetary Boundaries, Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs, Circular Economy – Human Development Approach

Title: Meaning-making processes in successful commercial games: A Dark Souls and Disco Elysium case study
Abstract/Summary: Games have developed the last decade into a strong and versatile medium with a strong potential and opportunities for societal change. Serious games have attempted to varying success to use the medium to create educational gain in their players, however, these games tend to create superficial experiences that don’t seem to retain the attention of the player. Commercial video games on the other hand focus on these deeper emotional experiences, but to what extent these experiences lead to meaningful implications towards the player’s real life, which are sought in serious games, is unexplored in literature. This research set out to explore these potential meaningful implications in the successful commercial games Dark Souls and Disco Elysium, using meaning making theory. Various meaningful themes have been found in both games, varying from empowerment and perseverance to diving into and understanding one’s own thoughts. These themes revolved around immersion and resonance with the real world, which led to a strong emotional involvement and thereby the lessons learned in the game were more easily translated towards the player’s real life.

Title: Sustainable Aviation Fuel Implementation Strategies for European Airports
Abstract/Summary: As the ambitious ReFuelEU mandate comes into force, the aviation sector faces uncertainties about its development. The main obligations currently fall upon fuel suppliers and aircraft operators, but airports can play a relevant role in the successful deployment of Sustainable Aviation Fuels (SAF). This research provides a guide to help airports understand their role and implement strategies in line with the ReFuelEU mandate. It investigates the role of airports within the fuel supply chain and their relationship to the mandate and SAF deployment. The research argues that airports can act as facilitators and advocates for SAF uptake. The guide outlines five steps for airports to create strategic roadmaps for SAF implementation, emphasizing the need for multi-stakeholder collaboration.
Main Research Question: What strategies can airports implement to support the uptake of Sustainable Aviation Fuels in line with the ReFuelEU mandate?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on the role of airports in the deployment of Sustainable Aviation Fuels, which directly contributes to reducing greenhouse gas emissions in the aviation sector, aligning with the goals of Climate Action.
Keywords: SAF, ReFuelEU, role of airports, SAF implementation in airports, SAF uptake

Title: The Steering Effects of the SDGs on Environmental and Developmental NGOs in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: This thesis examines the influence of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) on environmental and developmental NGOs in the Netherlands. The aim is to identify the steering effects of the SDGs on these organizations. The methodology includes the analysis of official NGO information and semi-structured interviews with experts in the field. The findings reveal that developmental NGOs experience stronger steering effects, both discursive and normative, compared to environmental NGOs, which face limitations due to political circumstances and organizational characteristics.
Main Research Question: What are the steering effects of the SDGs on environmental NGOs based in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: The SDGs emphasize the importance of partnerships among various stakeholders, including NGOs, to achieve sustainable development, which is central to the thesis.
Keywords: Sustianable Development Goals (SDGs), Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs), Environmental NGOs, Developmental NGOs, Steering Effects, Discursive Effects, Normative Changes
Specific Methods: Semi-structured interviews, NGO Website Content, Document analysis

Title: Circular Start-Ups in the Textile Industry: Adoption of Circular Practices and Context Barriers
