Main Research Question: How does participatory budgeting influence environmental justice in the context of urban green spaces in Wrocław, Poland?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on improving urban green spaces and ensuring equitable access for all communities, addressing the challenges of environmental justice.
Keywords: environmental justice, participatory budgeting, urban green spaces, procedural justice, distributive justice, recognitional justice

Title: The Social Acceptance of Renewable Energies and Climate-Neutral Electricity Mixes in Bavaria
Abstract/Summary: Social acceptance presents a significant barrier to the development of renewable energy (RE) despite widespread support for the energy transition. This research aims at exploring socio-political, community, and market acceptance of key technologies crucial for achieving a climate-neutral electricity supply in Bavaria, while also investigating preferred future electricity mixes. A mixed-method research approach was applied, combining desk research with quantitative and qualitative empirical data collection. The findings indicate that the recent energy crisis has shifted the primary motivation for the energy transition in Bavaria from climate protection to energy independence, while also increasing acceptance levels across all dimensions.
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on the acceptance of renewable energy technologies and the transition to a climate-neutral electricity supply.
Flag: Missing Research Question, Abstract too short

Title: Systems Thinking for Degrowth: The case of European aviation
Abstract/Summary: Degrowth is a burgeoning social movement and academic field calling for a democratic reduction in material and energy throughput to address environmental issues while ensuring well-being for all. This research builds on existing degrowth policy literature by using systems thinking tools to identify dominant feedback loops and leverage points for degrowth in the European aviation sector. Based on 25 expert interviews and an expert workshop, the study presents a causal loop diagram illustrating 15 feedback loops and identifies aviation industry lobbying and airline ticket prices as strategic intervention points for degrowth policies. The findings contribute to the development of effective degrowth transition strategies for European aviation.
Main Research Question: What are the dominant feedback loops and leverage points for degrowth in the European aviation sector?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the environmental issues associated with the growth of the aviation sector, which contributes significantly to climate change.
Keywords: Degrowth, Systems Thinking, European Aviation, Feedback Loops, Leverage Points
Specific Methods: Expert interviews, Expert workshop: Participatory systems mapping, Causal loop diagram

Title: Rural Youth Acting as Catalysts Exploring the role of ecochamps in facilitating co-management processes in the Umzimvubu Water Catchment, in South Africa
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the role of ecochamps in the Umzimvubu Water Catchment, South Africa, focusing on their influence in co-management processes. The aim is to understand how rural youth can act as catalysts for community engagement and decision-making. A mixed-methods approach was employed, utilizing household surveys, focus group discussions, and stakeholder interviews to gather data. The findings reveal that ecochamps significantly enhance community empowerment and knowledge integration, although challenges remain in achieving effective joint decision-making.
Main Research Question: How do ecochamps facilitate co-management processes in the Umzimvubu Water Catchment?
Justification for SDG: This SDG is relevant as the research focuses on water management and the role of local communities in sustainable practices within the water catchment area.
Keywords: eco-champs, co-management, community empowerment, knowledge integration, youth agency
Specific Methods: Household surveys, Focus group discussions, Stakeholder interviews

Title: Beyond Mining: Collective Imagination and Community Cohesion in Roșia Montană
Abstract/Summary: This study examines the relationship between collective imagination and social cohesion in Roșia Montană, a UNESCO World Heritage Site deeply impacted by a proposed mining project. The primary objective is to explore how socio-economic and environmental disruptions, particularly those caused by the Roșia Montană Gold Corporation (RMGC) project, have influenced local imaginaries and community cohesion. Using an ethnographic approach, the research integrates desk research, in-depth interviews, and participant observation conducted over nine months. The analysis reveals that the improper closure of the Cetate Quarry and the socio-political struggle related to the RMGC project have fragmented social cohesion, resulting in diverse and often conflicting local imaginaries. Despite numerous challenges, grassroots movements and local initiatives continue to foster a renewed sense of community and envision sustainable development.
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the impacts of mining on community cohesion and the importance of inclusive approaches to community building, which aligns with the goal of making cities and human settlements inclusive, safe, resilient, and sustainable.
Keywords: Social cohesion, Collective imagination, Post-mining development, Mine closure, Imagination deinfrastructuring
Specific Methods: Ethnographic approach, In-depth interviews, Participant observation

Title: Collaboratively classifying remote sensing imagery for Land Cover maps of Aruba
Abstract/Summary: Land cover changes are vital in shaping ecosystems, impacting food production, carbon sequestration, and essential services. This thesis focuses on creating land cover maps for Aruba, a small island nation facing environmental pressures from rapid urbanization. Accurate land cover maps can assist policymakers in addressing these challenges by providing essential data for sustainable land management. The study tackles challenges in developing these maps by selecting suitable land cover classes and determining the best machine learning classifiers. The results showed that K-Nearest Neighbours was the best-performing classifier, achieving an accuracy of 70.49%. These maps provide valuable insights for sustainable infrastructure planning.
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on land cover changes and their implications for ecosystems and sustainable land management in Aruba.
Keywords: Land cover classification, Hierarchical classification, landscape ethnoecology, remote sensing, machine learning classification, Sustainable Development Goals, Kunming-Montreal global biodiversity framework
Specific Methods: Machine learning classification, Google Earth Engine application

Title: Investigating the worlds of activists for virtual world design
Abstract/Summary: This study explores Indian activists’ worlds and their efforts at shaping their worlds. This research took an open approach to exploring the worlds and narratives of Indian environmentalists. After gaining insights, the study answered how these insights can be translated and presented in games that entail virtual worldbuilding. This research objective was fulfilled by closely working with a game-developing team. The study concludes that the interviewees engage in observation, learning, and discernment to refine their mental models. The study underlines the importance of supporting and understanding these agents of change and emphasizes that virtual worldbuilding must be approached sensitively to the cultures, people, and environments it draws inspiration from.
Keywords: World-making, worldbuilding, designing virtual worlds, realities, game industry, sustainability science, game and media impact

Title: Environmental justice considerations in urban greenspace planning: Insights from the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: This study examines how urban planners pursue environmental justice in urban greenspace planning processes in Dutch cities, focusing on Utrecht and Rotterdam. The research aims to map out how planners conceptualize, prioritize, and apply environmental justice in greening interventions. By conducting a comparative embedded multiple case study, including interviews and policy document analysis, the study reveals that environmental justice considerations are often implicit and limited, with a predominant focus on distributional aspects. The findings highlight the need for a more explicit acknowledgment of various justice dimensions in urban planning.
Main Research Question: How is environmental justice pursued in urban greenspace planning processes in Dutch cities?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the planning of urban greenspaces, which are essential for creating sustainable and equitable urban environments.
Keywords: Environmental justice dimensions, environmental justice framework, environmental justice trade-offs, urban greenspace planning
Specific Methods: Comparative embedded multiple case study, interviews, analysis of policy documents

Title: Analysing the distributional effects of low emission zones. A case study of the city of Barcelona.
Abstract/Summary: This thesis addresses the integration of social justice into sustainability policies, focusing on Low Emission Zones (LEZ) in urban areas, particularly Barcelona. The aim is to examine the distributional effects of LEZ on different income groups. The methodology employs an embedded single-case study design, combining quantitative correlation analysis, qualitative surveys, and expert interviews. Findings indicate that lower-income households disproportionately experience negative impacts from LEZ policies, facing challenges in adaptation and increased economic burdens. The study advocates for equitable LEZ implementation that enhances accessibility and reduces strain on vulnerable populations.
Main Research Question: What are the distributional effects of Low Emission Zones on different income groups in Barcelona?
Justification for SDG: The study highlights the need for equitable urban policies that address both environmental goals and social justice, aligning with the aim of making cities inclusive and sustainable.
Keywords: Low Emission Zones, social justice, sustainability policies, Barcelona, distributional effects
Specific Methods: Statistical correlation analysis, qualitative surveys, semi-structured interviews

Title: Ownership: what role does it play in ensuring a just transition?
Abstract/Summary: Transitioning away from fossil fuels towards renewable energy is essential for carbon neutrality. This thesis investigates the role of ownership in energy production and distribution in ensuring a just transition, using the Netherlands as a case study. It identifies different ownership forms and examines their effects on energy justice, focusing on distributional, procedural, and recognition justice. Data was collected through literature reviews and interviews with stakeholders in the Dutch energy field. The findings indicate that ownership significantly influences the justice of the energy transition, with various ownership forms having different implications. The study highlights barriers faced by more 'just' ownership forms and suggests that policy-makers should consider these implications for future energy justice.
Main Research Question: What role does ownership of energy production and distribution play in ensuring a just transition?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the thesis focuses on the transition to renewable energy and the implications of ownership structures on energy justice, which are crucial for achieving affordable and clean energy for all.

Title: Linking faunal seed dispersal to tree dispersal traits and carbon storage in southern African woodlands
Abstract/Summary: This research investigates the relationship between faunal seed dispersal, tree dispersal traits, and carbon storage in southern African woodlands, particularly in the context of climate change mitigation. The study aims to understand how human-induced defaunation affects carbon storage by analyzing tree inventory, carbon, and dispersal traits across four southern African countries. Five new SEOSAW plots were established in Madagascar for data collection. The analysis revealed significant positive relationships between above-ground biomass, seed size, and fruit size, while human disturbance negatively impacted carbon storage and seed dispersal traits. The findings highlight the need for further research due to gaps in trait data.
Main Research Question: How does faunal seed dispersal influence tree dispersal traits and carbon storage in southern African woodlands?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on the role of forests in carbon storage and the impact of biodiversity loss on ecosystem processes.
Keywords: Carbon storage, above-ground biomass, wood density, faunal seed dispersal, savanna woodlands, tree traits
Specific Methods: Statistical analysis, Spearman rank correlations, principal component analysis, linear regressions, community-weighted means

Title: Governance Conditions for Successful Restoration of Riverine Ecosystems: A Case Study of the Rhine River
Abstract/Summary: This research focuses on analysing the governance conditions for successful restoration of riverine ecosystems. To derive these conditions, firstly a scientific literature assessment is conducted to compile relevant governance conditions for ecosystem restoration into an analytical framework. This framework is then tested and refined by conducting a critical case study on the ecosystem restoration process of the Rhine River to bring back the Atlantic salmon, as coordinated by the International Commission for the Protection of the Rhine (ICPR). The analysis reveals strengths and weaknesses in the governance structure, indicating the need for improvement in restoration measures and suggesting potential gaps in governance conditions.
Main Research Question: What are the governance conditions necessary for the successful restoration of riverine ecosystems, specifically in the context of the Rhine River?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the restoration of riverine ecosystems, which is crucial for maintaining biodiversity and ecosystem services, aligning with the goal of protecting, restoring, and promoting sustainable use of terrestrial ecosystems.
Keywords: Ecosystem restoration, Atlantic Salmon, Rhine, ICPR, Governance, Riverine
Specific Methods: Case study, Expert interviews, Policy analysis
Organizations Studied: International Commission for the Protection of the Rhine

Title: Adapting to Scarcity Community-based water management and climate adaptation: Insights from Santa Cruz canton, Guanacaste, Costa Rica
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores community-based water management and its role in climate adaptation in Santa Cruz, Guanacaste, Costa Rica. The aim is to understand local perceptions of water scarcity and the effectiveness of current management strategies. A mixed methods approach was employed, including literature review, stakeholder analysis, and interviews with local residents and experts. Findings indicate that while community-based approaches have potential, significant barriers remain, including governance issues and socio-cultural factors that hinder effective adaptation.
Main Research Question: How can community-based water management contribute to climate adaptation in Santa Cruz canton, Guanacaste, Costa Rica?
Justification for SDG: This SDG is relevant as the research focuses on water management practices and their adaptation to climate change, addressing the critical need for sustainable water resources.
Keywords: Community-based water management, climate adaptation, water scarcity, Santa Cruz, Guanacaste

Title: How Much is Enough?: Redistributive Power for a Just Energy Transition
Abstract/Summary: This research explores the role of community energy initiatives in the energy transition, emphasizing the need for democratization and redistribution of energy provisioning. The aim is to support practitioners in engaging with energy justice through a conceptual framework grounded in capabilities, intersectionality, and spatiality. The study employs extensive interviews with stakeholders from energy communities in the Netherlands, Sweden, and the United Kingdom, leading to the development of a question matrix to facilitate reflection on distributive energy justice. The findings highlight the potential of local initiatives to contribute to energy justice and provide a dashboard tool for practitioners to assess the impacts of energy communities.
Main Research Question: How can community energy initiatives understand, actively reflect on, and engage with their contributions towards distributive energy justice?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on community energy initiatives that aim to democratize energy access and promote energy justice, aligning with the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.

Title: Fostering Legume Cultivation in the Netherlands: A Mission-Oriented Innovation System Analysis
Supervisor: Dr. James Patterson, Dr. Julia Tschersich
Abstract/Summary: The Netherlands faces several critical sustainability challenges in its agricultural system, including excessive nitrogen emissions and a heavy reliance on imported legumes. This thesis investigates the systemic barriers and enables scaling legume cultivation in the Netherlands, aligning with national and European ambitions for a protein transition. The study applies the mission-oriented innovation system framework to analyse the adoption of legume cultivation through qualitative methodology, including policy document analysis and semi-structured interviews. The results reveal significant progress in stakeholder alignment but highlight critical systemic barriers that impede innovation development. This research contributes to the theoretical development of the MIS framework and offers policy recommendations for fostering innovation in sustainable agriculture.
Keywords: Agricultural Transitions, Barriers and Enablers, Legume Cultivation, Mission-Oriented Innovation Systems, Protein Transition

Title: Innovative Financing Strategies for Involving the Private Sector in the Mainstreaming of Nature-Based Solutions: A Case Study in Barcelona
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the role of innovative financing strategies involving the private sector in mainstreaming Urban Nature-Based Solutions (UNBS) in Barcelona. It examines two municipal programs: green roofs and green walls, each utilizing distinct financing strategies that blend public and private funds. The research identifies barriers to financing and assesses how these strategies address issues such as information gaps and undervaluation of natural capital. Findings indicate that while both strategies have made progress, challenges remain, particularly regarding perceived risks and economic returns. The study emphasizes the importance of context-specific approaches and collaborative networks for successful implementation.
Main Research Question: How do innovative financing strategies that involve the private sector contribute to the mainstreaming of Urban Nature Based Solutions?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on urban nature-based solutions that enhance sustainability and resilience in cities, directly contributing to the goal of making cities inclusive, safe, resilient, and sustainable.
Keywords: Cities, Urban Nature-Based Solutions, Innovative finance, Innovative financing strategies.

Title: The Political Effects of Sustainable Development Goals on Sustainable Consumption and Production Efforts of United Nations Environment Programme
Abstract/Summary: The United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) has promoted sustainable consumption and production (SCP) initiatives. This research investigates how the launch of Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 12 influences UNEP’s SCP-related discourse, norms, and institutions. The study employs document analysis and quantitative text analysis of 74 UNEP documents, alongside 11 semi-structured interviews. Findings reveal that SDG 12 provides UNEP with a new common language and legitimizes its SCP efforts, enhancing stakeholder support. However, limitations within SDG 12 and UNEP's institutions hinder further institutional effects. This research contributes to global governance studies and offers insights for monitoring SDG 12's impact.
Main Research Question: How does the launch of SDG 12 influence UNEP’s SCP-related discourse, norms, and institutions?
Justification for SDG: SDG 12 is central to promoting sustainable consumption and production patterns, which UNEP actively supports through its initiatives.
Keywords: Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), SDG 12 (Sustainable Consumption and Production), United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP), Discursive Effects, Normative Effects, Institutional Effects, Causal Mechanism.
Specific Methods: Document analysis, Quantitative text analysis, Semi-structured interviews

Title: The Role of Power Electronics for the Raw Material Demand of the Energy Transition
Supervisor: Wolfgang Eichhammer, Patrick Hercegfi, Henrike Köhler
Abstract/Summary: The global energy system is transitioning to renewable energy to phase out fossil fuels and combat climate change. This thesis analyses the role of power electronics for the raw material demand of the energy transition, focusing on devices necessary for energy generation technologies, energy storage, and electric vehicles. A scenario analysis on both a global and a German level was conducted based on the material demand of six representative devices. The findings indicate that the material demand for power electronics will multiply in the coming years, particularly for copper, aluminium, and iron, with electric vehicles having the highest demand. Key technological developments include wide bandgap semiconductors and increased circularity through recycling.
Main Research Question: What is the role of power electronics in the raw material demand of the energy transition?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the thesis addresses the material demand and potential bottlenecks associated with the energy transition, emphasizing the importance of sustainable resource management.
Keywords: Power electronics, energy transition, raw material demand, renewable energy, electric vehicles

Title: Cement Demand Forecasting: Developing a Comprehensive Conceptual Framework for Forecasting Cement Demand in EU27 countries
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the dynamics of cement demand within the EU27 region, aiming to identify key drivers that policymakers can target to facilitate a successful transition toward more sustainable practices in the cement industry. The study analyses historical trends and correlations in cement demand driver indicators, focusing on long-term energy models. A comprehensive framework for forecasting cement demand was developed and tested through a case study in the Netherlands. The findings highlight the complex interplay between socio-economic factors and cement demand, emphasizing the need for policymakers to incentivize sustainable practices.
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on the cement industry, which is a critical sector for infrastructure development and innovation in sustainable practices.
Specific Methods: Case study, Trend analysis, Correlation analysis, Expert interview
Flag: Missing Research Question, Abstract too short

Title: Bagasse Utilization and Management Strategies in São Paulo Sugarcane Mills
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the utilization of sugarcane bagasse in São Paulo state, Brazil. Bagasse, a byproduct of sugarcane processing, is a crucial source of bioelectricity in Brazil, with variations in its use across different mills due to differences in cogeneration efficiency. The study examines current and potential utilization of sugarcane bagasse to determine the feasibility of freeing up bagasse. The findings suggest that by adopting strategies such as improving cogeneration efficiency and utilizing sugarcane straw, substantial amounts of bagasse could be freed up for other uses without compromising its essential role in energy generation.
Main Research Question: How is sugarcane bagasse used in São Paulo state of Brazil and how it can be possible to increase its availability for commercialization without compromising its current role in the sugarcane industry?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the thesis focuses on improving the utilization of sugarcane bagasse for bioelectricity, contributing to renewable energy sources and energy efficiency in Brazil.
Keywords: sugarcane, bagasse, bioelectricity, Brazil, sustainability

Title: Assessing the political impact of UN Sustainable Development Goals on the central government of Taiwan
Abstract/Summary: This thesis examines the political impact of the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) on Taiwan's central government, despite Taiwan's non-membership in the UN. It aims to analyze the cause-and-effect relationships between the UN-SDGs and Taiwan's national policies. Utilizing a single case study design, qualitative content analysis was conducted on 41 official documents and 22 interview transcripts. Findings reveal that Taiwan's formulation of its Sustainable Development Goals (T-SDGs) was influenced by its international status and domestic political environment, leading to institutional changes and the adoption of diplomatic strategies aligned with UN-SDGs. This research contributes to understanding the unique political dynamics of sustainable development in Taiwan.
Main Research Question: What political changes have occurred in Taiwan's SD since the launch of UN-SDGs in 2015, and what factors have contributed to these changes?

Title: Challenging radical democracy to go lower: How commoning practices and collaborative governance in the Gipuzkoa Region enact radical democracy on the micro- and meso-level
Abstract/Summary: As liberal democracies are struggling with declining public trust and are failing to create adequate solutions to the climate crisis, one alternative is radical democracy that seeks to radicalise the democratic values of pluralism, equality and liberty throughout existing institutions and domains of nations and economies. This research has tested if commoning practices enact radical democracy on the micro-level and if collaborative governance enacts radical democracy on the meso-level. A case study of the Gipuzkoa Region is used to test the applicability of the theories as supplements for the theory of radical democracy. The research has found that commoning practices in Gipuzkoa enact radical democracy on the micro-level through lived alternative democratic relationships. Further, the collaborative governance in Gipuzkoa enacts radical democracy through institutions and democratic practices that allow for more pluralism through participation.
Keywords: Radical democracy, commoning practices, collaborative governance, Basque Country, Gipuzkoa

Title: Trippy Transformation: Exploring the potential role of psychedelics in achieving sustainable development at a deeper systems level
Abstract/Summary: The dominance of Capitalist systems within Western societies have resulted in paradigms and ontologies which are not only unsustainable but are actively destructive to the well-being of people and the planet. This research aimed to empirically investigate whether and how psychedelic experiences result in sustainability-relevant changes at the individual level and to explore how individuals with psychedelic experiences can contribute to sustainable transformations at the collective level. A qualitative analysis of 18 in-depth interviews was conducted, revealing positive changes in ways of being related to emotions, spirituality, and actions within the world. The results indicate that psychedelics may act as catalysts for shifting paradigms towards more sustainable alternatives.
Main Research Question: How do psychedelic experiences result in sustainability-relevant changes at the individual level and how can individuals with psychedelic experiences contribute to sustainable transformations at the collective level?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research explores how psychedelic experiences can lead to changes in individual behaviors and paradigms that promote sustainability and responsible consumption.
Keywords: Serotonergic psychedelics, leverage points, inner transformation-sustainability nexus, paradigms and ontological transformations

Title: Governance for Adoption of Agroecological Practices in Hass Avocado Production: Case study of San Vincente de Ferrer, Colombia.
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the governance structures influencing the adoption of agroecological practices in Hass avocado production in San Vicente de Ferrer, Colombia. The aim is to identify the necessary conditions for adoption and the factors affecting these conditions. A qualitative empirical research methodology was employed, including case study analysis and interviews with local stakeholders. The findings reveal that specific governance arrangements significantly impact the adoption of agroecological practices, highlighting the need for tailored governance strategies to enhance sustainability in agricultural practices.
Main Research Question: To what extent are the conditions necessary for adoption of agroecological practices present in the case study?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on sustainable agricultural practices and the governance needed to promote responsible production methods in avocado farming.
Keywords: Agroecology, Governance, Sustainable Practices, Avocado Production, Colombia

Title: Effects of soil water regime and ECM inoculant on soil’s N & P availability and the aboveground physiological structure of Pinus brutia seedlings
Abstract/Summary: The Calabrian trees (Pinus brutia) are negatively affected by climate change due to prolonged and intense fires, as well as extended drought, especially in the summer. This study aimed to investigate the effect of ectomycorrhizal (ECM) spores’ inoculation on four-months-old Pinus brutia seedlings, focusing on aboveground development and nutrient uptake. Sixty large pots were prepared with four treatment groups regarding irrigation and inoculation. Results indicated that ECM inoculation significantly affected shoot height growth and nutrient absorption, suggesting that early inoculation enhances resilience to abiotic stresses and influences aboveground development.
Main Research Question: What is the effect of ectomycorrhizal inoculation on the aboveground development and nutrient uptake of Pinus brutia seedlings?

Title: Harvesting Innovation: Navigating Enablers and Challenges for Chilean Startups on the Path to Sustainable Food Ecosystems
Abstract/Summary: The global food and agriculture industry faces multiple challenges in production, demand and regulations. Known as the largest industry worldwide, this industry plays a vital role in Latin America as the breadbasket for its significant contribution to the global production and export of agricultural and fish commodities. This thesis emphasizes the critical need to reevaluate and transform existing systems. It explores the concepts of food sustainability and food ecosystem, examining how a comprehensive transformation in food production and consumption can lead to a broader shift towards sustainability. Addressing the complexities of the food ecosystem involves collaboration across various levels with multiple stakeholders and their dynamics. Therefore, this research explores the role of AgriFoodTech startups and their ecosystem, in Chile specifically, in developing innovations that enable transition pathways towards transformed systems.
Keywords: Food Sustainability, Food Ecosystem, AgriFoodTech startups, Startups ecosystem, Multi-Level Perspective framework, Enabler Theory, Chile

Title: Consumer Behaviour, Rebound Effects, and LCA-research: Analysing Environmental Benefits of Reuse of Denim Jeans and Button Shirts in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: The textile industry is among Earth’s most polluting industries, leading to significant CO2 emissions and waste creation. This study aims to assess the environmental benefits of clothing reuse through Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), focusing on consumer behaviour and rebound effects (REs). A comparative environmental assessment was conducted in the Netherlands, analyzing linear use versus reuse of denim jeans and button shirts. The study involved literature research, interviews with store managers and consumers, and survey data. Findings indicate that reusing denim jeans and button shirts can lead to substantial resource and environmental savings. However, the sample size was insufficient to generalize RE occurrences, highlighting the need for further research.
Main Research Question: To what extent do environmental benefits occur when consuming second-hand clothing compared to consuming new clothing bought from fast fashion stores, when also considering rebound effects?
Justification for SDG: This study addresses the environmental impacts of clothing consumption and promotes sustainable practices in the textile industry, aligning with the goal of responsible consumption and production.

Title: The Body and Post-Extractivism An exploration of the role of embodiment in the development of post-extractivist imaginaries
Abstract/Summary: The global economy depends substantially on the material resources extracted from the environment. Extractivism is a process of intensive extraction of natural resources, which are used as energy sources or materials for producing goods that tend to exceed the capacity of the environment to regenerate itself. Recently, the concept expanded to mean a mentality that enables the exploitation and exhaustion of the other-than-human. As a response to a growing concern with the scale and unsustainability of extractivism, a new concept has been developed: post-extractivism. This exploratory study combined art and science to investigate how artistic embodiment practices can facilitate the development of post-extractivist imaginaries. My main results indicated that artistic embodiment engaging with dialogue and attunement has the potential to enable post-extractivist imaginaries.
Main Research Question: How can artistic embodiment practices facilitate the development of post-extractivist imaginaries?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the unsustainable practices of extractivism and explores alternatives that promote responsible consumption and production through post-extractivist imaginaries.
Keywords: extractivism, post-extractivism, embodiment, imaginaries, other-than-human

Title: What are the effects of restoration measures on plant types in a degraded raised bog: The Fochteloërveen case study
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the impact of innovative restoration methods, specifically peat inversion, on Sphagnum recovery and Molinea reduction in the Fochteloërveen bog remnant. Utilizing drone surveying combined with Random Forest classification, two sites that underwent peat inversion in 2000 and 2023 were compared with a control site. Results indicated that the 2000 inverted site did not have more Sphagnum or less Molinea cover when compared to the control, although biomass density in the 2000 inverted site was higher. The 2023 inverted site predominantly consisted of open water and high levels of dead Molinea due to the recent peat inversion. Ultimately, this research confirms that the monitoring of bog restoration efforts using drones and modern classification techniques can be accurate and effective.
Keywords: Wetlands, Restoration, Bogs, Peat, Random Forest, Drone Survey, Classification, Peat Inversion, UAS, Sphagnum, Molinea

Title: Navigating the Margins of Urban Sustainability Transitions Environmental Degradation in Metropolitan Barranquilla
Abstract/Summary: Urban spaces are currently at the spotlight of sustainability transitions. More and more researchers and policy makers are drawing attention to the role that cities have on climate change, as well as the potential they have of reducing environmental degradation by promoting urban sustainability transitions. However, these transitions can sometimes neglect or worsen pre-existing socio-environmental problems. The city of Barranquilla is currently spearheading urban sustainability transitions in Colombia, with its new Ecopark, green spaces, and renewable energy infrastructures. Meanwhile, at the South of Barranquilla, the city of Malambo has been experiencing for decades a severe environmental degradation of its wetlands. This degradation is still under researched, and even less is known about the connections between the situation in Malambo and the urban sustainability transition in Barranquilla. Therefore, this master thesis attempts to characterize this socio-environmental degradation in Malambo, within the context of these increasing investments and policies for developing a sustainable Barranquilla. To do so, an ethnographic research was conducted, according to - and guided by - a decolonial ecology perspective. This investigation aims to raise more attention to what is happening in Malambo, while at the same time contributing to a more critical view about urban sustainability transitions. It is crucial to bear in mind that promises of salvation in such transitions do not erase inequalities and injustices of the past. Hence, this research expects to raise discussions on what extent urban transitions may also lean on such inequalities to sustain themselves.
Keywords: Urban sustainability transitions, Socio-environmental degradation, Decolonial ecology, Water-based livelihoods

Title: The case of nuclear phase-out postponement in Belgium: atomic habits?
Abstract/Summary: This research maps the political debate on nuclear phase-out postponement in Belgium from 1999 to 2022, aiming to understand the role of nuclear techno-optimism in climate and energy policy-making. Initially, Belgium planned to deactivate nuclear power plants by 2025, but extensions have postponed this to 2035. Using the Advocacy Coalition Framework, the study analyzes the policy subsystem and tests the hypothesis that techno-optimism has led to climate delay. Findings indicate that nuclear techno-optimism influenced policy outputs, but other factors also contributed to the postponement.
Main Research Question: How has the discourse of nuclear techno-optimism impacted policy-output of nuclear phase-out postponement in Belgium between 1999 and 2022?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the impact of nuclear techno-optimism on climate policy, highlighting the urgency of effective climate action in the context of energy production.
Keywords: nuclear energy, techno-optimism, climate delay, discourse coalitions, ecological modernization
Specific Methods: Discourse network analysis, policy document analysis, semi-structured interviews
